news_summary: Recent scientific discoveries are advancing our understanding of chronic pain mechanisms, innovative dietary approaches for mental health, the evolutionary origins of human eyes, and cellular evolution through mitochondrial activity.
news_summary_fr: Les récentes découvertes scientifiques nous permettent de mieux comprendre les mécanismes de la douleur chronique, les approches alimentaires innovantes en matière de santé mentale, les origines évolutives de l'œil humain et l'évolution cellulaire à travers l'activité mitochondriale.
news_summary_es: Los recientes descubrimientos científicos están ampliando nuestro conocimiento sobre los mecanismos del dolor crónico, los enfoques dietéticos innovadores para la salud mental, los orígenes evolutivos del ojo humano y la evolución celular a través de la actividad mitocondrial.

articles.title: Scientists may have found the brain’s switch for chronic pain
articles.summary: Deep within the brain, scientists have uncovered a hidden “switch” that may decide whether pain fades away—or lingers for months or even years. Researchers found that a small, little-known region called the caudal granular insular cortex (CGIC) acts like a command center, telling the body to keep pain signals alive long after an injury has healed. In animal studies, shutting down this pathway not only prevented chronic pain from forming but could even erase it once it had taken hold.
articles.content:
New research from the University of Colorado Boulder points to a little-known brain circuit that may determine whether short-term pain fades away or becomes a long-lasting problem. The findings suggest that this pathway plays a key role in turning temporary pain into chronic pain that can persist for months or even years.

The study, conducted in animals and published in the Journal of Neuroscience, focused on a region called the caudal granular insular cortex (CGIC). Researchers found that shutting down this circuit can both prevent chronic pain from developing and stop it after it has already begun.

"Our paper used a variety of state-of-the art methods to define the specific brain circuit crucial for deciding for pain to become chronic and telling the spinal cord to carry out this instruction," said senior author Linda Watkins, distinguished professor of behavioral neuroscience in the College of Arts and Sciences. "If this crucial decision maker is silenced, chronic pain does not occur. If it is already ongoing, chronic pain melts away."

New Tools Driving a "Gold Rush of Neuroscience"

The work comes at a time of rapid progress in brain research. First author Jayson Ball describes the current moment as a "gold rush of neuroscience," driven by advanced tools that allow scientists to precisely control specific groups of brain cells.

With these techniques, researchers can now pinpoint the exact neural pathways involved in complex conditions like chronic pain. This level of detail could help guide the development of new treatments, including targeted infusions or brain-machine interfaces, which may offer safer alternatives to opioid medications.

"This study adds an important leaf to the tree of knowledge about chronic pain," said Ball, who earned his doctorate in Watkins' lab in May and now works for Neuralink, a California-based startup that develops brain-machine interfaces for human health.

When Pain Signals Don't Shut Off

Chronic pain is a widespread issue. According to the Centers for Disease Control, about one in four adults experience it, and nearly one in 10 say it interferes with their daily lives.

A common feature of nerve-related pain is allodynia, a condition in which even light touch can feel painful.

Short-term and long-term pain behave very differently. Acute pain acts as a warning signal, starting when injured tissue, such as a stubbed toe, sends messages through the spinal cord to the brain. Chronic pain, however, continues even after the injury has healed, creating a kind of false alarm that can last for weeks, months, or years.

"Why, and how, pain fails to resolve, leaving you in chronic pain, is a major question that is still in search of answers," said Watkins.

Targeting the Brain Pathway That Sustains Pain

Earlier work from Watkins' lab in 2011 pointed to the CGIC as an important player in pain sensitivity. This small region, about the size of a sugar cube, is located deep within the insula, a part of the brain involved in processing sensations. Studies in people have shown that this area tends to be overactive in those with chronic pain.

Until recently, studying this region in detail was difficult because the only way to affect it was to remove it, which is not a realistic treatment option.

In the new study, the team used fluorescent proteins to track which nerve cells became active after a rat experienced a sciatic nerve injury. They then applied advanced "chemogenetic" methods to turn specific genes on or off within selected neurons.

Their results showed that the CGIC is not very important for handling immediate pain but is essential for keeping pain going over time.

How the Brain Keeps Pain Alive

The researchers found that the CGIC sends signals to the somatosensory cortex, the part of the brain that processes touch and pain. This area then communicates with the spinal cord, effectively instructing it to continue transmitting pain signals.

"We found that activating this pathway excites the part of the spinal cord that relays touch and pain to the brain, causing touch to now be perceived as pain as well," said Ball.

When the scientists turned off this pathway shortly after injury, the animals experienced only brief pain. In cases where chronic pain had already developed, disabling the circuit caused the pain to stop.

"Our research presents a clear case that specific brain pathways can be directly targeted to modulate sensory pain," said Ball.

Toward New Treatments for Chronic Pain

Researchers still do not know what triggers the CGIC to begin sending persistent pain signals, and more studies are needed before these findings can be applied to people.

Even so, the work points toward new possibilities for treatment. Ball envisions a future where doctors use targeted injections or infusions to affect specific brain cells without the widespread side effects and risk of addiction associated with opioids. He also suggests that brain-machine interfaces, whether implanted or worn externally, could help manage severe chronic pain.

"Now that we have access to tools that allow you to manipulate the brain, not based just on a general region but on specific sub-populations of cells, the quest for new treatments is moving much faster," he said.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough identifying a specific brain circuit responsible for chronic pain, with potential to prevent or reverse chronic pain conditions. This discovery has broad implications for millions suffering from chronic pain and could lead to safer, more effective treatments, addressing a major public health issue.
articles.personality_title: Scientists discover brain circuit that controls chronic pain
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Chronic pain affects millions of people and can last long after an injury has healed. Unlike normal pain, which warns the body about damage, chronic pain continues without a clear cause, making it hard to treat.

**What happened** – Researchers at the University of Colorado Boulder found a small brain area called the caudal granular insular cortex (CGIC) that acts like a switch to keep pain signals active. In animal tests, turning off this brain circuit stopped chronic pain from starting and even erased it after it had formed.

**Impact** – This discovery is important because it shows a clear brain pathway that controls whether pain becomes long-lasting. It could lead to new treatments that target this brain area, offering relief without the risks of current pain medicines like opioids.

**What's next step** – Scientists need to learn what triggers this brain circuit to start sending ongoing pain signals. Future research will also explore treatments that use injections or brain-machine devices to safely control chronic pain in people.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists identified a brain circuit that acts as a switch for chronic pain, opening the door to new ways to prevent and treat long-lasting pain conditions.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques découvrent le circuit cérébral qui contrôle la douleur chronique
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La douleur chronique touche des millions de personnes et peut durer longtemps après la guérison d'une blessure. Contrairement à la douleur normale qui alerte le corps d’un dommage, la douleur chronique persiste sans cause claire, ce qui la rend difficile à traiter.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’Université du Colorado à Boulder ont découvert une petite zone du cerveau appelée cortex insulaire granulaire caudal (CGIC) qui agit comme un interrupteur pour maintenir les signaux de douleur actifs. Lors d’expériences sur des animaux, désactiver ce circuit cérébral a empêché la douleur chronique de commencer et l’a même effacée après son apparition.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle montre un circuit cérébral clair qui contrôle si la douleur devient durable. Elle pourrait ouvrir la voie à de nouveaux traitements ciblant cette zone du cerveau, offrant un soulagement sans les risques des médicaments actuels comme les opioïdes.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques doivent comprendre ce qui déclenche ce circuit cérébral pour envoyer des signaux de douleur persistants. Les recherches futures viseront aussi à développer des traitements utilisant des injections ou des dispositifs cerveau-machine pour contrôler en toute sécurité la douleur chronique chez l’humain.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les scientifiques ont identifié un circuit cérébral qui agit comme un interrupteur pour la douleur chronique, ouvrant la voie à de nouvelles méthodes pour prévenir et traiter la douleur durable.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos descubren circuito cerebral que controla el dolor crónico
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El dolor crónico afecta a millones de personas y puede durar mucho tiempo después de que una lesión haya sanado. A diferencia del dolor normal, que avisa al cuerpo de un daño, el dolor crónico continúa sin una causa clara, lo que dificulta su tratamiento.

Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad de Colorado en Boulder encontraron una pequeña zona del cerebro llamada corteza insular granular caudal (CGIC) que funciona como un interruptor para mantener activos los mensajes de dolor. En pruebas con animales, apagar este circuito cerebral evitó que el dolor crónico comenzara e incluso lo eliminó después de que se había desarrollado.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento es importante porque muestra un camino cerebral claro que controla si el dolor se vuelve duradero. Podría llevar a nuevos tratamientos que apunten a esta zona del cerebro, ofreciendo alivio sin los riesgos de los medicamentos actuales como los opioides.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos deben entender qué activa este circuito cerebral para enviar señales persistentes de dolor. Las investigaciones futuras también explorarán tratamientos con inyecciones o dispositivos cerebro-máquina para controlar el dolor crónico de forma segura en personas.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Los científicos identificaron un circuito cerebral que actúa como un interruptor para el dolor crónico, abriendo la puerta a nuevas formas de prevenir y tratar el dolor prolongado.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a glowing neural pathway shaped like a delicate, twisting ribbon of light connecting a small, intricately textured brain region resembling a sugar cube deep within a stylized brain silhouette, with gentle pulses of light traveling along the pathway toward a softly illuminated spinal cord depicted as an elegant column, all rendered in natural, muted earth tones and soft blues to evoke calm and healing.

articles.title: Why the keto diet could be a revolutionary way to treat mental illness
articles.summary: You may think of the high-fat, low-carb eating plan as a faddish way to lose weight. But the keto diet is now being used to tackle conditions from severe depression to bipolar disorder and anorexia, with transformative results
articles.content:
You may think of the high-fat, low-carb eating plan as a faddish way to lose weight. But the keto diet is now being used to tackle conditions from severe depression to bipolar disorder and anorexia, with transformative results

Brett Ryder

In February, Robert F. Kennedy Jr., the US secretary of health, made a characteristically bold claim. A doctor at Harvard University, he proclaimed, “has cured schizophrenia using keto diets”.

If you happened to be passing Harvard University that day, you might have heard the sound of that doctor’s palm hitting his forehead. “For the record, I have never ever once used the word ‘cure’ in any of my work,” says Christopher Palmer, a psychiatrist at Harvard Medical School. “Nonetheless, I have used the word ‘remission’…”

The idea that a diet best known as a fat-busting fad could treat serious mental illness might sound like the latest offering from the wild west of online wellness: something destined to be filed alongside raw water and coffee enemas in the annals of terrible suggestions.

But there are a number of reasons why the idea of using the diet for conditions affecting the brain deserves closer inspection. For one thing, over 100 years’ worth of research has shown that ketogenic diets have real, measurable effects on the body in general, as well as on the organ between your ears. For another, many of these changes – some at the cellular level, others throughout the whole body – are known treatment targets in mental illness.

With evidence from small trials and case studies indicating that ketogenic diets can dramatically improve symptoms in some people with mental health conditions, a growing number of scientists are wondering if what is called metabolic psychiatry could lead to much-needed new treatments for conditions affecting the brain and the mind-body connection.

“We’ve wasted 30 years thinking about dopamine and serotonin as the sole targets for psychiatric medications,” says Daniel Smith, a psychiatrist at the University of Edinburgh, UK. “This is a new paradigm. It’s exciting.”

Fuelling the brain

Nowadays, ketogenic diets are mainly associated with efforts to lose weight, but they were first developed more than a century ago to treat problems with the brain. At that time, few drugs were available for epilepsy. One thing that did seem to work was fasting for several days at a time, which significantly reduced seizures and sometimes stopped them altogether. The problem was, it wasn’t sustainable. Sooner or later, people would need to eat and when they did, their seizures would return.

Researchers set out to find a solution, and in the 1920s, Russell Wilder, a physician and epilepsy researcher at the Mayo Clinic in Minnesota, cracked it. He developed a high-fat, moderate-protein and very-low-carb diet to mimic fasting, while still providing enough energy to live on. He called it the ketogenic diet because it was designed to trigger the metabolic shift that happens when the body cannot get hold of carbohydrates from food and so has to switch to burning fats, generating small molecules called ketone bodies in the process.

As we are a species that mostly eats plants, carbohydrates are our default fuel. Carbs are quickly broken down into glucose during digestion, which can be burned in our cells for energy. This process takes place in the mitochondria, cellular organelles where food is converted into adenosine triphosphate (ATP), the body’s energy currency. What isn’t used straight away is stored as glycogen in the liver and muscles, to be called on between meals. When those stores are full, any spare calories are deposited as fat.

If food is short, and carbohydrates aren’t so easy to come by, the body reverses the process. First it releases stored glycogen, which can keep us ticking over for up to a day. When that runs out, the body begins to break into its fat reserves for energy.

Some of the fatty acids released are sent to be burned in the mitochondria, while others are passed to the liver, where they are converted into ketone bodies. Ketone bodies are smaller than fatty acids and, because they are water-soluble, are easier to transport in the blood to where they are needed. They also have the advantage of being small enough to cross the blood-brain barrier, allowing them to be used in place of glucose as fuel for the brain.

The keto diet is high-fat, moderate-protein and low-carb – making foods like eggs, meat, avocado and leafy greens typical fare Addictive Stock/StockFood

It is an ingenious system, and the switch back and forth between fuel types probably came into play pretty regularly for our hunter-gatherer ancestors. For most modern humans, though, carbs are so easy to come by that the metabolic switch happens rarely, if at all.

Wilder’s idea was for a diet designed to flick the switch to fat burning, while providing enough fat in the diet so the body didn’t need to break down its own fat reserves. If this state of “nutritional ketosis” worked, it could be used as a more sustainable alternative to fasting.

In 1921, Wilder published a paper showing that it did indeed work. In three people with epilepsy, the ketogenic diet reduced seizures as effectively as fasting, and could be maintained for longer. Later research backed him up and the keto diet became an epilepsy treatment. When new anticonvulsant medications came along in the 1930s, though, Wilder’s diet fell out of favour, only to be used in young children and those who don’t respond to any available medications.

Nevertheless, the fact that it worked suggested that there’s something about “going keto” that corrects problems with brain function. Several decades of research later, we have a better idea of what’s happening under the lid when our bodies switch to back-up-fuel mode.

The short answer is: lots of things. The simplest and most obvious is that it involves eating far less sugar. While glucose is our body’s default energy source, having too much of it is known to be disastrous for the health of the body and brain. Long-term overindulging in carbs contributes to inflammation, insulin resistance, diabetes and obesity, while the evidence suggests that high-sugar diets are more likely to lead to low mood in people both with and without depression.

It is possible, then, that some of the keto diet’s effects come down to bringing us closer to the diet our bodies evolved to expect. “We are wired to like sugar, but in nature, sugar wasn’t around that much,” says Guido Frank, a psychiatrist at the University of California, San Diego. “It’s not that sugar is [always] bad for us, it’s about the quantity.”

One upshot of reducing carbs is a change in the microbiome. Ketogenic diets have been shown to inhibit the growth of carb-loving, pro-inflammatory bacteria in the gut, which may reduce inflammation throughout the body and brain. The well-known link between the microbiome and mental health, and the role of the gut-brain axis in regulating brain function, may also play a role in improving symptoms.

Some of these effects may be possible without going full keto. The classic ketogenic diet involves reducing carbohydrates from 45 per cent of total calories to just 1 to 5 per cent. Reducing carbs less dramatically, or following a low-glycaemic-index diet that focuses on slow-release carbohydrates instead of simple sugars, might provide at least some of the benefits, says Palmer. “A low-glycaemic-index diet is not necessarily ketogenic, but it’s certainly having anti-inflammatory and insulin-signalling effects and probably affects the gut microbiome as well,” he says.

Other changes, however, do seem to require switching to ketosis. One example is the way ketone bodies appear to act directly on the brain to balance two key neurotransmitters: glutamate, which excites neurons to fire, and GABA, which inhibits their firing. An excess of glutamate, relative to GABA, is associated with the uncontrolled firing seen in epileptic seizures and the erratic brain activity implicated in psychosis. Some of the anticonvulsant medications that are used to treat epilepsy, schizophrenia and bipolar disorder boost GABA relative to glutamate. Evidence from mouse studies suggests that keto diets do something similar.

Ketone bodies

How exactly ketone bodies rebalance these neurotransmitters isn’t clear. One possibility, however, is that they simply provide enough energy for the brain to do its job properly. Ketone bodies are harder to produce in the body than glucose, but, once formed, are a more efficient fuel source for the mitochondria, netting 27 per cent more ATP per molecule.

A boost of energy in the brain could do more than just even out neurotransmitter levels. Studies focusing on schizophrenia, bipolar disorder and depression, as well as Alzheimer’s disease and anorexia nervosa, have found evidence of mitochondrial dysfunction. And while there are many routes to malfunctioning mitochondria – from genetics to diet and other lifestyle factors – it does suggest that a problem with releasing energy from glucose may play a role in many brain-related issues.

Carmen Sandi, a neuroscientist at the Swiss Federal Institute of Technology in Lausanne, studies the connection between mitochondrial health and mental illness. She points out that the brain is the most energy-hungry organ, demanding 20 per cent of the body’s fuel at rest, despite only accounting for 2 per cent of body weight. But “that’s only part of the story”, she says.

Ketone bodies are tiny enough to cross the blood-brain barrier, which means they can be used as fuel for the brain, in place of glucose JAMES BELL/SCIENCE PHOTO LIBRARY

“Mitochondria are not just the energy producers – they also contribute to the precise functioning of neurons and brain circuits”, with a role in making hormones and other signalling molecules, and in regulating inflammation and managing oxidative stress. There is some evidence that ketone bodies generate less oxidative stress than glucose, says Sandi, so reduce the demand for metabolic clean-up.

For Palmer, all of this evidence points to a common underlying cause for mental health conditions: that they are due to metabolic problems in the brain. He points to the fact that physical metabolic conditions, such as diabetes, obesity and insulin resistance, substantially increase the risk of depression. The reverse is also true: people with mental health conditions are at greater risk of diabetes, obesity and heart disease.

That ketogenic diets might help treat metabolic problems in the brain first occurred to Palmer in the late 2010s when he was treating a women in her 70s who had experienced debilitating, drug-resistant schizophrenia for more than 50 years.

As is common in severe mental illness, in addition to being extremely mentally unwell, the woman, Mildred, had obesity and her physical health was deteriorating. She was advised to try the keto diet to lose weight. Within weeks, in addition to weight loss, she started to notice improvements in her schizophrenia symptoms. The voices in her head became quieter, her mood improved and, then, after decades of being affected by the condition, she went into full remission. Palmer was amazed, and in 2019 published a paper describing Mildred’s experience and a similar remission result from a second person with schizophrenia.

It was this research that caught the attention of Robert F. Kennedy Jr., and spawned a grassroots movement spearheaded by the philanthropist Jan Ellison Baszucki and her husband David Baszucki, founder and CEO of the tech company Roblox. In 2021, the couple’s son, Matthew, had been struggling with treatment-resistant bipolar disorder for five years. Having run out of other treatment options, he went on the ketogenic diet, under the guidance of Palmer. Within months, he, too, was in remission. The family went on to set up a foundation to fund research in metabolic psychiatry and to share stories from people who have had a positive experience with keto. At the last count, the foundation’s Metabolic Mind YouTube account had more than 97,000 subscribers.

When eating keto, carbohydrates like bread are firmly off the menu Patrick Chatelain/www.plainpicture.com

Meanwhile, research by other groups added to the excitement, including one 2022 study in 31 people, all of whom had previously been hospitalised with severe depression, bipolar disorder or schizoaffective disorder. Of the 31 volunteers who agreed to try the ketogenic diet, 28 managed to stick to it for two weeks or more. All 28 saw some improvement in their symptoms and almost half reached the criteria for clinical remission. However, the study had no control group.

If there is one mental health condition where a fat-burning diet wouldn’t seem to apply, it would be anorexia. Suggesting that people who have a history of disordered eating cut a major food group out of their diet sounds irresponsible and potentially dangerous.

Yet evidence shows that, when people are properly supported and under medical supervision, ketogenic diets might just help. Frank was involved in a 2022 pilot study in five people with anorexia that reported that they were able to maintain a healthy weight on the ketogenic diet with fewer food-related anxieties. Four of the five kept the diet up after the study and continued to improve. “It was incredible,” says Frank. “I hadn’t seen that before in 20 years of working in this field.”

As with other mental health conditions, there are plausible reasons why going keto might help. For one, studies by Cynthia Bulik, a clinical psychiatrist at the Karolinska Institute in Sweden, and her colleagues have shown that anorexia is associated with gene variants linked to inefficient energy release in the mitochondria.

As such, one possibility is that when someone with a genetic metabolic vulnerability starts dieting, the switch to ketosis brings a boost of energy and lowers anxiety. This, in turn, may reinforce the compulsion to restrict food further, trapping people in a cycle of weight loss that gets out of control. “People with anorexia nervosa may have a goal weight, but for many of them, it’s never enough,” says Frank. “It’s almost like an addictive process.”

Now, researchers are wondering if a properly balanced ketogenic diet could help people with anorexia to find a delicate balance – allowing them to get to a healthy weight while removing the compulsion to restrict food. If going into nutritional ketosis can reduce anxiety in a similar way to self-starvation, then “mimicking the underweight state by providing ketone bodies for energy creation might make the need to restrict unnecessary”, says Frank.

It is early days, says Sahib Khalsa, a psychiatrist who researches and treats anorexia at the University of California, Los Angeles. He adds that keto diets shouldn’t be tried for mental health conditions without a doctor’s support. “There’s a difference between trying it with an eating disorder psychiatrist who’s monitoring you carefully, and reading about a ketogenic diet and then deciding spontaneously to do it,” he says. “From a safety standpoint, I think it’s definitely premature for that.”

Filling the gaps

For all the case studies and testimonials, keto for the brain is far from a done deal. Smith points out that there have been no properly controlled, randomised trials in large numbers of individuals, so it is impossible to know what proportion of people will respond as dramatically as Mildred and Matthew Baszucki. “There might be two people who have an excellent response, and that’s great,” he says. “But there might be 98 other people who don’t get any response.”

To try to fill this gap, Smith, along with Steven Marwaha at the University of Birmingham, UK, is beginning a large-scale, randomised controlled trial in 200 people with bipolar depression. The study will compare a nutritional ketogenic diet with a diet based on UK healthy eating guidelines. The results won’t be in for at least five years, says Smith. In the meantime, the results of a pilot study with 27 people that Smith and his colleagues published in 2025 were encouraging, finding a correlation between ketone levels and improved mood and energy levels. Brain imaging also showed a decrease in glutamate levels in brain regions involved in emotional processing.

Another unknown is whether any of the many mechanisms are more important than others for people who might benefit from the keto diet. “It is plausible that different individuals derive benefit through different dominant mechanisms, depending on their underlying metabolic and neurobiological vulnerabilities. However, in many cases, the therapeutic effects likely arise from their combined impact,” says Shebani Sethi, a metabolic psychiatrist at Stanford University in California.

As larger clinical trials get under way, and the results continue to roll in, metabolic psychiatry could go a number of ways. Keto diets might turn out to work very well for some people, and not at all for others, in which case research will focus on identifying markers that indicate who they might benefit. Or further research may allow us to extract the special sauce from ketogenic diets, which could then inform new drug treatments and make it unnecessary to stick to a restrictive diet.

For now, though, says Palmer, the important thing is to make people aware that, even when they have tried everything, there is still hope. “Some people respond really well to existing treatments, but it’s heartbreaking to see patients year after year, decade after decade, who are doing everything we’re asking them to do and they are profoundly suffering. Offering even one additional tool that might work for some patients is my passion. This is our moment for metabolism and mental health. That’s what I’m hoping.”
articles.reasoning: The article reports on promising evidence that the ketogenic diet, a well-established metabolic approach, may offer transformative benefits for serious mental illnesses such as schizophrenia, bipolar disorder, depression, and anorexia. It highlights real-world cases of remission and ongoing scientific research, indicating a potential new paradigm in mental health treatment with broad significance for public health. The article provides substantial context on the diet's history, mechanisms, and clinical trials, making it a substantive and hopeful health breakthrough story.
articles.personality_title: Ketogenic diet shows promise in treating serious mental illnesses
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The ketogenic diet, known for being high in fat and low in carbohydrates, was originally developed in the 1920s to treat epilepsy. Today, it is mainly popular for weight loss. However, new research suggests it may help with mental health conditions like schizophrenia, bipolar disorder, depression, and anorexia.

**What happened** – Doctors and scientists have observed cases where people with severe mental illnesses improved significantly after following the keto diet. For example, a woman with drug-resistant schizophrenia experienced remission after starting the diet. Other studies have found that many patients with serious mood disorders saw symptom improvements when they followed keto. Researchers are now conducting larger trials to better understand these effects.

**Impact** – This is important because mental illnesses are often hard to treat, and many people do not get better with current medications. The keto diet works by changing the body’s energy source from sugar to fat, producing ketone bodies that fuel the brain more efficiently. This may help balance brain chemicals and reduce inflammation, which are linked to mental health problems. If confirmed, keto could offer a new way to treat mental illness that focuses on metabolism, not just brain chemicals.

**What's next step** – Scientists are running large, controlled studies to see who benefits most from the keto diet and why. They also want to find out if it’s possible to develop new treatments based on how the diet affects the brain without needing to follow the strict eating plan. Meanwhile, experts warn that people should only try keto for mental health under medical supervision.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The ketogenic diet, long used for epilepsy, is now showing real potential as a new tool to improve symptoms in serious mental illnesses by changing how the brain uses energy.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le régime cétogène montre un potentiel dans le traitement des maladies mentales graves
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le régime cétogène, riche en graisses et pauvre en glucides, a été créé dans les années 1920 pour traiter l’épilepsie. Aujourd’hui, il est surtout connu pour la perte de poids. Mais de nouvelles recherches suggèrent qu’il pourrait aider à soigner des troubles mentaux comme la schizophrénie, le trouble bipolaire, la dépression et l’anorexie.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des médecins ont observé des cas où des personnes atteintes de maladies mentales sévères se sont nettement améliorées après avoir suivi ce régime. Par exemple, une femme souffrant de schizophrénie résistante aux médicaments est entrée en rémission grâce au régime. D’autres études montrent que beaucoup de patients avec des troubles de l’humeur graves ont vu leurs symptômes diminuer avec le keto. Des essais plus larges sont en cours pour mieux comprendre ces effets.

**Impact** – C’est important car les maladies mentales sont souvent difficiles à traiter et de nombreuses personnes ne s’améliorent pas avec les médicaments actuels. Le régime keto change la source d’énergie du corps, passant du sucre aux graisses, ce qui produit des corps cétoniques qui nourrissent mieux le cerveau. Cela pourrait aider à équilibrer les substances chimiques du cerveau et réduire l’inflammation, liées aux troubles mentaux. Si cela est confirmé, le keto pourrait offrir une nouvelle approche basée sur le métabolisme.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs mènent de grandes études contrôlées pour déterminer qui bénéficie le plus du régime et pourquoi. Ils veulent aussi savoir s’il est possible de créer de nouveaux traitements basés sur ces effets sans suivre un régime strict. En attendant, les experts recommandent de ne pas commencer ce régime sans surveillance médicale.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Le régime cétogène, utilisé depuis longtemps contre l’épilepsie, montre aujourd’hui un réel potentiel pour améliorer les symptômes de maladies mentales graves en modifiant la façon dont le cerveau utilise l’énergie.
articles.personality_title_es: La dieta cetogénica muestra potencial para tratar enfermedades mentales graves
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La dieta cetogénica, alta en grasas y baja en carbohidratos, fue creada en la década de 1920 para tratar la epilepsia. Hoy en día, es más conocida por ayudar a perder peso. Sin embargo, nuevas investigaciones indican que podría ayudar a tratar enfermedades mentales como la esquizofrenia, el trastorno bipolar, la depresión y la anorexia.

**Qué pasó** – Médicos y científicos han visto casos donde personas con enfermedades mentales graves mejoraron mucho tras seguir la dieta cetogénica. Por ejemplo, una mujer con esquizofrenia resistente a medicamentos entró en remisión después de comenzar la dieta. Otros estudios muestran que muchos pacientes con trastornos del estado de ánimo graves mejoraron sus síntomas con keto. Ahora se están realizando estudios más amplios para entender mejor estos efectos.

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque las enfermedades mentales suelen ser difíciles de tratar y muchas personas no mejoran con los medicamentos actuales. La dieta keto cambia la fuente de energía del cuerpo del azúcar a la grasa, produciendo cuerpos cetónicos que alimentan el cerebro de forma más eficiente. Esto puede ayudar a equilibrar químicos cerebrales y reducir la inflamación, factores relacionados con problemas mentales. Si se confirma, keto podría ofrecer una nueva forma de tratamiento basada en el metabolismo.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos están llevando a cabo estudios controlados grandes para descubrir quiénes se benefician más y por qué. También quieren saber si se pueden crear nuevos tratamientos basados en estos efectos sin seguir la dieta estricta. Mientras tanto, los expertos advierten que las personas solo deben probar keto para salud mental con supervisión médica.

**Frase clave** – La dieta cetogénica, usada desde hace tiempo para la epilepsia, muestra potencial real como nueva herramienta para mejorar síntomas de enfermedades mentales graves al cambiar cómo el cerebro usa la energía.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a glowing human brain gently cradled within an open avocado half, surrounded by scattered eggs and leafy greens, with soft, natural earth tones highlighting the transformation from darkness to light inside the brain, symbolizing healing and metabolic renewal through the ketogenic diet.

articles.title: The shocking origin of human eyes traces back to an ancient “cyclops”
articles.summary: A bizarre, cyclops-like creature from nearly 600 million years ago may hold the key to how your eyes—and even your sleep cycle—evolved. Scientists have discovered that all vertebrates, including humans, trace their vision back to a single light-sensitive “median eye” perched atop a worm-like ancestor’s head. As this ancient animal shifted from a sedentary to a more active lifestyle, it lost and then reinvented its vision, eventually giving rise to the paired, image-forming eyes we rely on today.
articles.content:
Humans share a surprisingly strange ancestor with all other vertebrates. New research suggests that far back in evolutionary history, one of our earliest relatives had a single eye, much like a tiny cyclops, positioned on top of its head.

Scientists from Lund University and the University of Sussex report that all vertebrates can be traced back to this ancient, one-eyed organism. According to their findings, the remains of that original "median eye" still exist today, but in a very different form. It has become the pineal gland, a small structure deep in the brain.

"The results are a surprise. They turn our understanding of the evolution of the eye and the brain upside down," says Dan-E Nilsson, professor emeritus in sensory biology at Lund University.

A Worm-Like Creature From 600 Million Years Ago

This distant ancestor lived nearly 600 million years ago. It was a small, worm-like animal that spent most of its time in one place, feeding by filtering plankton from seawater. Earlier in its evolutionary history, it likely had two eyes, similar to many other animals.

"We don't know whether the paired eyes in our branch of the evolutionary tree were just light-sensitive cells or simple image-forming eyes. We only know that the organism later lost them," says Dan-E Nilsson.

As the creature adapted to a more stationary lifestyle, having two eyes was no longer necessary. Over time, those paired eyes disappeared.

How a Single "Median Eye" Took Over

Even after losing its paired eyes, the organism retained a cluster of light-sensitive cells in the center of its head. These cells gradually formed a simple, single eye that could detect light and darkness and help the animal sense orientation.

Millions of years later, this ancestor became more active again and returned to a swimming lifestyle. That shift created new pressure to develop better vision. Researchers believe that parts of the original median eye eventually gave rise to new, paired eyes capable of forming images.

Why Vertebrate Eyes Are So Different

This unusual evolutionary path helps explain why vertebrate eyes are built differently from those of other animals like insects and squid.

"Now we finally understand why the eyes of vertebrates differ so radically from the eyes of all other animal groups, such as insects and squid. The film of our eyes -- the retina -- developed from the brain, whereas the eyes of insects and squid originate in the skin on the sides of the head," says Dan-E Nilsson.

In vertebrates, the retina is essentially an extension of the brain. In contrast, many other animals develop eyes from surface tissues on the body.

A Detour That Shaped Modern Vision

Researchers describe this history as an unusual evolutionary detour. Instead of evolving directly from earlier eye structures, vertebrate vision appears to have been rebuilt from this single median eye after the loss of earlier paired eyes.

This conclusion comes from detailed comparisons of light-sensitive cells across different animal groups, as well as their structure and location within the body.

"For the first time, we now also understand the origin of the neural circuits that analyze the image in our retina," adds Dan-E Nilsson.

The Pineal Gland: A Living Remnant of an Ancient Eye

One of the most intriguing discoveries is that a piece of this ancient eye still exists in humans today. It has evolved into the pineal gland, a light-sensitive organ in the brain.

The pineal gland produces melatonin, a hormone that helps control the body's circadian rhythm, including sleep patterns.

"It's mind-boggling that our pineal gland's ability to regulate our sleep according to light stems from the cyclopean median eye of a distant ancestor 600 million years ago," concludes Dan-E Nilsson.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery about the evolutionary origin of human eyes and the pineal gland, which regulates sleep cycles. This breakthrough enhances understanding of human biology and evolution with broad implications for health and science, providing inspiring knowledge about our origins and biological functions.
articles.personality_title: Scientists trace human eyes back to a single ancient ‘cyclops’ ancestor
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Nearly 600 million years ago, a small worm-like animal lived with a very unusual feature: a single eye in the middle of its head. This creature is now believed to be the ancestor of all vertebrates, including humans.

**What happened** – Researchers from Lund University and the University of Sussex discovered that this ancient animal first had two eyes but lost them as it became less active. Later, it developed a simple single eye, called a median eye, which helped it sense light. Over millions of years, this median eye evolved into the paired, image-forming eyes vertebrates have today. The original median eye also became the pineal gland in our brains, which helps control sleep.

**Impact** – This discovery changes how scientists understand eye evolution. It explains why vertebrate eyes are different from those of insects or squid. Our eyes developed from brain tissue, while others come from skin. The finding also connects the pineal gland’s role in sleep to this ancient eye, showing a deep link between vision and daily body rhythms.

**What's next step** – Scientists will continue studying how the brain and eyes developed together. Understanding this evolution could help improve knowledge about eye diseases and how sleep is controlled. It may also inspire new research on how animals see and adapt to their environments.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Human eyes and the sleep-regulating pineal gland both evolved from a single ancient eye on a distant ancestor’s head nearly 600 million years ago.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques retrouvent l’origine des yeux humains chez un ancien « cyclope »
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Il y a près de 600 millions d’années, un petit animal ressemblant à un ver possédait un œil unique au milieu de la tête. Cet animal serait l’ancêtre de tous les vertébrés, y compris les humains.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs des universités de Lund et Sussex ont découvert que cet ancien animal avait d’abord deux yeux, mais les a perdus en devenant moins actif. Puis, il a développé un œil simple, appelé œil médian, qui détectait la lumière. Au fil du temps, cet œil médian a évolué pour donner les yeux jumelés formant des images que nous connaissons aujourd’hui. Cet œil est aussi à l’origine de la glande pinéale dans notre cerveau, qui régule le sommeil.

**Impact** – Cette découverte change notre compréhension de l’évolution des yeux. Elle explique pourquoi les yeux des vertébrés sont différents de ceux des insectes ou des calmars. Nos yeux viennent du cerveau, tandis que les autres proviennent de la peau. Le lien entre la glande pinéale et cet œil ancien montre aussi une connexion profonde entre la vision et le rythme quotidien du corps.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques vont continuer à étudier comment le cerveau et les yeux ont évolué ensemble. Cela pourrait aider à mieux comprendre les maladies des yeux et le contrôle du sommeil. Cela pourrait aussi inspirer de nouvelles recherches sur la vision et l’adaptation des animaux.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les yeux humains et la glande pinéale qui régule le sommeil proviennent d’un œil unique sur la tête d’un lointain ancêtre vieux de près de 600 millions d’années.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos rastrean los ojos humanos hasta un antiguo ‘cíclope’
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Hace casi 600 millones de años, un pequeño animal parecido a una lombriz tenía un solo ojo en el centro de su cabeza. Este animal es el ancestro de todos los vertebrados, incluidos los humanos.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de las universidades de Lund y Sussex descubrieron que este antiguo animal primero tenía dos ojos, pero los perdió al volverse menos activo. Luego desarrolló un ojo simple, llamado ojo medio, que detectaba la luz. Con el tiempo, este ojo medio evolucionó para formar los ojos dobles que vemos hoy. También se convirtió en la glándula pineal, que regula el sueño.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo cambia la forma en que entendemos la evolución de los ojos. Explica por qué los ojos de los vertebrados son diferentes a los de insectos o calamares. Nuestros ojos vienen del cerebro, mientras que los otros vienen de la piel. Además, muestra la conexión entre la glándula pineal y el ojo antiguo, relacionando la visión con el ritmo diario del cuerpo.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos seguirán estudiando cómo evolucionaron el cerebro y los ojos juntos. Esto podría ayudar a entender mejor las enfermedades oculares y el control del sueño, y también inspirar nuevas investigaciones sobre la visión y adaptación animal.

**Resumen en una frase** – Los ojos humanos y la glándula pineal que regula el sueño evolucionaron de un único ojo en la cabeza de un ancestro hace casi 600 millones de años.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a small, ancient worm-like creature with a single large eye centered on its head, resting peacefully on a soft ocean floor surrounded by gentle plankton; above it, a subtle glowing pineal gland shape is embedded within a translucent, abstract brain silhouette, symbolizing the evolutionary link between the ancient median eye and modern human biology.

articles.title: Mitochondria can spawn new ‘organelles’ — hinting at how modern cells evolved
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 27 April 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01295-0Discovery bolsters theory that ancient mitochondria formed brand-new, specialized ‘sacs’ in cells by shedding their outer membranes.
articles.content:
Researchers are discovering that mitochondria (blue), known as cells’ power plants, also play parts in cells’ immune responses.Credit: David M. Phillips/Science Photo Library

When a parasite invades a cell, the cell’s mitochondria react by shedding their outer layers to form brand-new cellular compartments — or organelles — that digest molecular trash.

Cells are swapping their mitochondria. What does this mean for our health?

The team that made the discovery showed that when the mitochondria — best known as cells’ energy producers — form these organelles, it helps the parasite to proliferate, although it isn’t clear exactly how. Maybe the parasite ‘feeds’ off the degraded material inside the tiny compartments, says team leader Lena Pernas, an immunologist at the University of California, Los Angeles.

But one thing is certain, Pernas said when presenting the findings at a Keystone symposium on mitochondrial signalling, held in Colorado in February: “Mitochondria are able to give rise to new organelles during infection.”

The discovery, posted on 24 April to the preprint server bioRxiv1 ahead of peer review, adds to a growing list of roles that researchers are uncovering for mitochondria in immunity, including surveilling pathogens2 and coordinating immune signalling3. It also lends credence to the hypothesis that membrane sacs, or vesicles, shed by the earliest mitochondria gave rise to organelles in eukaryotes — a group of organisms, including plants and animals, whose cells have an enclosed nucleus and other intracellular compartments. If modern-day mitochondria can spawn new organelles, which have specialized jobs inside cells, it’s easy to imagine that their ancestors did, too.

Mitochondria manipulation

Pernas and her colleagues observed the formation of the organelles after infecting human cancer cells with the parasite Toxoplasma gondii, which can lurk in undercooked meat. A protein on the outer surface of the parasite latched onto a protein on the cells’ mitochondria, ‘pinning’ the tiny energy producers to T. gondii. These tethered mitochondria then began shedding their outer membranes, forming what are called structures positive for outer mitochondrial membrane (SPOTs). Remarkably, the SPOTs went on to engulf other organelles in the cells called lysosomes, which are acidified sacs that act as waste-disposal systems.

In this video, watch a large lysosome (red, near the centre of the screen) be enveloped by a SPOT (yellow).Credit: Dr. Xianhe Li

This envelopment of the lysosomes is quite the feat, says Shaeri Mukherjee, a cell biologist and immunologist at the University of California, San Francisco. “What is so cool and surprising is the ability of a pathogen to completely, not only manipulate the mitochondria, but use the mitochondria to generate an entire new organelle in the cell, with such precision,” says Shaeri Mukherjee, cell biologist and immunologist at the University of California, San Francisco.

The researchers confirmed that the SPOTs-engulfed lysosomes are a new type of organelle, different from lysosomes: the outer surfaces of the new organelles don’t bear any proteins typically expressed by lysosomes. They also demonstrated that to multiply, T. gondii needs the new organelles. When the researchers used a drug called a proton-pump inhibitor to prevent the SPOTs-engulfed lysosomes from acidifying — and therefore becoming waste-disposal systems — the parasite’s proliferation was impaired.

Interestingly, the SPOTs rarely engulf any other organelles inside cells other than acidified lysosomes. One reason the parasites might create the new organelles is to feed off their digested waste, Pernas says. Another could be that, by forcing the SPOTs to engulf lysosomes, T. gondii neutralizes the tiny waste disposers, which are harmful to pathogens, Pernas says.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery about mitochondria forming new organelles during infection, which advances understanding of cellular biology and immune response. This insight could have broad implications for health, particularly in understanding and potentially controlling parasitic infections like Toxoplasma gondii. The discovery is detailed, focused, and represents a meaningful breakthrough in biomedical science with potential future benefits for disease treatment.
articles.personality_title: Scientists discover mitochondria can create new cell parts during infection
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Mitochondria are tiny parts inside cells that produce energy. Scientists have been learning that mitochondria also help cells fight infections.

**What happened** – Researchers found that when a parasite called Toxoplasma gondii infects human cells, mitochondria change by shedding their outer layer. This creates new parts inside the cell, called organelles, which capture and break down waste. The parasite uses these new organelles to grow and multiply.

**Impact** – This is the first time scientists have seen mitochondria form new organelles during infection. It helps explain how cells can respond to threats and supports ideas about how complex cells evolved long ago. Understanding this process may lead to better ways to stop parasites that cause illness.

**What's next step** – Scientists want to learn exactly how these new organelles help parasites grow. They also hope to find drugs that can block this process, which could lead to new treatments for infections caused by Toxoplasma gondii and similar parasites.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Mitochondria can create new organelles during infection, revealing a new way cells respond to parasites and offering clues about cell evolution and disease control.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les mitochondries peuvent créer de nouveaux organites lors d’une infection
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les mitochondries sont de petites parties à l’intérieur des cellules qui produisent de l’énergie. Les scientifiques découvrent qu’elles aident aussi les cellules à combattre les infections.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs ont trouvé que lorsqu’un parasite appelé Toxoplasma gondii infecte des cellules humaines, les mitochondries perdent leur couche externe. Cela crée de nouvelles parties à l’intérieur de la cellule, appelées organites, qui capturent et détruisent les déchets. Le parasite utilise ces nouveaux organites pour grandir et se multiplier.

**Impact** – C’est la première fois que l’on observe que les mitochondries forment de nouveaux organites pendant une infection. Cela explique comment les cellules réagissent aux menaces et confirme des idées sur l’évolution des cellules complexes. Comprendre ce processus pourrait aider à mieux combattre les parasites responsables de maladies.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques veulent comprendre comment ces organites aident les parasites à se développer. Ils espèrent aussi trouver des médicaments pour bloquer ce mécanisme, ce qui pourrait permettre de nouveaux traitements contre les infections dues à Toxoplasma gondii et d’autres parasites.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – Les mitochondries peuvent créer de nouveaux organites lors d’une infection, révélant une nouvelle façon dont les cellules réagissent aux parasites et offrant des pistes sur l’évolution cellulaire et la lutte contre les maladies.
articles.personality_title_es: Las mitocondrias pueden crear nuevos orgánulos durante una infección
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las mitocondrias son partes pequeñas dentro de las células que producen energía. Los científicos están descubriendo que también ayudan a las células a luchar contra infecciones.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores encontraron que cuando un parásito llamado Toxoplasma gondii infecta células humanas, las mitocondrias pierden su capa externa. Esto crea nuevas partes dentro de la célula, llamadas orgánulos, que capturan y destruyen desechos. El parásito usa estos nuevos orgánulos para crecer y multiplicarse.

**Impacto** – Es la primera vez que se observa que las mitocondrias forman nuevos orgánulos durante una infección. Esto ayuda a entender cómo las células responden a amenazas y apoya ideas sobre cómo evolucionaron las células complejas. Comprender este proceso podría ayudar a encontrar mejores formas de detener parásitos que causan enfermedades.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos quieren saber exactamente cómo estos nuevos orgánulos ayudan al parásito a crecer. También esperan descubrir medicamentos que puedan bloquear este proceso, lo que podría llevar a nuevos tratamientos para infecciones causadas por Toxoplasma gondii y parásitos similares.

**Frase clave** – Las mitocondrias pueden crear nuevos orgánulos durante una infección, mostrando una nueva forma en que las células responden a parásitos y ofreciendo pistas sobre la evolución celular y el control de enfermedades.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a glowing blue mitochondrion gently shedding golden membrane layers that transform into a protective, intricate cellular compartment enveloping a vibrant red lysosome, symbolizing the birth of a new organelle inside a softly illuminated abstract cell environment.
